narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to forecast bar exam success on the first try, estimated a 67.8% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. For context, you're a Black male with an upper-middle-class family background, planning to attend full-time, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 is close to the typical 3.21 average across past students. However, three key factors stood out as the strongest influences on this prediction, with your LSAT score of 23.50—well below the average of 36.70—having the biggest push toward forecasting failure, as lower scores like yours signal challenges in the rigorous legal reasoning needed for the bar. Next in influence was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far below the average of about 5.5, which further tilted the model toward expecting struggles, since students in lower deciles historically face higher bar failure rates. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the strongest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward success because it aligns reasonably well with those of students who typically pass. Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your profile shows weaker early academic momentum and test performance that outweighed your solid college grades, putting you in a riskier category overall. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many students from similar circumstances thrive with targeted preparation—but the model weighs these patterns from past data heavily. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward trajectory. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on bolstering your profile, and explore prep resources tailored for bar success, like those supporting underrepresented students. With focused effort on these areas, you can reapply stronger next cycle. Your determination as a Black male pursuing law is inspiring, and many have turned similar predictions around through persistence.
